creator: rometravelers
caption:
Most people know the maritozzo as a cream-filled Roman pastry.

Far fewer know that it was once associated with courtship.💍🫶🏼

For centuries, young Roman men would give a maritozzo to the woman they hoped to marry. During Lent, when richer foods were restricted, these sweet buns became a special treat. Over time they became linked to engagements and future brides, and according to local tradition, some suitors even hid rings, coins, or small gifts inside.

The name itself comes from marito - husband.

In other words, long before diamond companies convinced the world that proposals required a small fortune, Rome already had its own edible love letter. Which in my humble opinion is very on brand for us 😏

And before anyone starts a war in the comments…

Ask the internet where to get a maritozzo and you’ll get the same three answers.

Ask a Roman and you’ll start a family feud.

Some of the names that repeatedly come up in local debates include:

• Grué
• Dolce Maniera
• De Bellis
• Cristalli di Zucchero
• Linari
• Andreotti
• Romoli
• Valentini
• Le Levain
• Siciliana Svizzera

The correct answer, of course, is to conduct extensive field research and eat several.

For historical accuracy.

If you enjoy the stories behind Rome’s food, streets, monuments, and traditions, there are longer conversations waiting elsewhere.

Follow me, I’ll lead the way.

transcription.text: Did you know that one of the most classic Roman snacks that you can get in the entire city is er maritozzo, the husband. Not too bad of a choice, is it? Sweet bread filled with whipped cream, this has been a staple since ancient Roman times. Gained massive popularity during the medieval times when the church allowed for this to be a part of Lent, normally a period of observing fasting. But in its simplicity, it was misleading, because who knew all the fluffy whipped cream goodness in this sweet doughy bread, what a pleasurable snack it can be. You can find it with pine nuts, you can find it with raisins, chocolates, with sake, or vegan, gluten-free, every single version under the sun. In the 19th century, it became tradition, nobody quite knows why, for future husbands to plant the ring inside the maritozzo and then present to, hopefully, their future wives-to-be. One of my favorite places in all of Rome to get a classic maritozzo is Regoli. It was started by three sisters in the early 20th century and it's still serving them off the door and people queue up as generations have done before them. If you want to know more cool places to see and history about Rome, follow me, I'll lead the way.
